title: Routing a condensing boiler condensate pipe to a drain
subtitle: Connect or re-route the acidic condensate discharge pipe to an internal or external drain
excerpt: How to route the condensate discharge pipe from a condensing boiler to a suitable drain. Covers the difference between internal and external discharge, pipe gradient, freeze protection for external pipes, and the regulations on condensate connection.
sourceNotes: Building Regulations Approved Document J: Combustion appliances and fuel storage systems (condensate disposal guidance). HHIC (Heating and Hot Water Industry Council) condensate guidance (publicly available). British Gas Engineers reference guide (format reference).

glossaryTerms.definition: The weakly acidic water (pH 3 to 5) produced as a by-product of a condensing boiler's heat exchanger. Formed when flue gases cool below the dew point. Condensate must be discharged to an approved drain — never into the soil stack directly without trapping, as it is not a sewage waste but a dilute acid that can corrode certain metal pipes. Discharged at a flow rate of approximately 2 to 3 litres per hour of boiler operation.

The minimum downhill slope required in a condensate pipe to ensure self-draining without pooling. The HHIC guidance specifies a minimum fall of 45 mm per metre (2.5 degrees). A pipe run without gradient creates a siphon trap that can prevent the boiler from clearing its condensate, causing the boiler to fault.

content.text: and needs a pipe to dispose of it. When a boiler is replaced or moved, the condensate pipe route usually needs to be extended or re-run. The regulations are straightforward: the preferred method is internal discharge to the internal soil stack, an internal drain, or an internal combined waste, with the pipe maintaining a
throughout. External discharge is acceptable but must be insulated against freezing.

22 mm push-fit plastic waste pipe (condensate pipe is typically 22 mm or 32 mm). Push-fit fittings (elbows, tee for stack connection). Pipe clips. A mini pipe cutter. For external pipe: 25 mm pipe lagging and weatherproof tape. For wall penetration: a 25 mm or 32 mm core drill bit. Silicone sealant for the wall penetration.

The preferred discharge hierarchy, per the approved guidance, is: (1) internal soil stack (via a trap); (2) internal floor drain; (3) combined household drain gulley; internal only; (4) external soil stack; (5) external gulley at ground level; (6) purpose-made soakaway. Do not discharge condensate to a rainwater drain, a header pipe in the roof space, or a gutter. On a new installation, always choose the highest available point in this list.
Step 2, plan and run the pipe

Plan the pipe route from the boiler outlet to the discharge point, maintaining a continuous fall of 45 mm per metre with no upward sections. Minimise bends; each 90-degree elbow reduces the effective gradient. Push-fit waste fittings are the standard material: cut lengths with the mini pipe cutter, deburr the cut end, and push fully home into the fitting socket. Clip the pipe at 500 mm centres to prevent it sagging, which would interrupt the gradient.

For a soil stack connection: fit a boss adaptor into the stack (a 22 mm or 32 mm rubber seal entry) and connect the condensate pipe to it, ensuring the connection point is below the highest trap outlet level on the stack to prevent siphonage. For a gulley connection: run the pipe to terminate above the grating but inside the gulley guard (not at the grate surface where it would freeze). Fit a sealing plate around the pipe entry through any external wall.

Any section of condensate pipe on an external wall or in an unheated space must be insulated with 25 mm thick pipe lagging and covered with weatherproof tape. Frozen condensate is the most common cause of boiler faults in cold snaps: the pipe freezes solid, the boiler cannot clear its condensate, and it shuts down on a fault code. If the external run is longer than 3 m, consider a wider pipe (32 mm) to reduce the risk of freezing.

attrs.symptom: Boiler faults with a condensate error code in cold weather
attrs.fix: Thaw the pipe with warm water (not boiling). The boiler will usually restart once the pipe clears. Insulate the external section properly to prevent recurrence.

attrs.symptom: Condensate dripping or pooling at a pipe joint
attrs.cause: Push-fit fitting not fully inserted; pipe not fully home in socket.
attrs.fix: Isolate the boiler. Dry the joint. Pull the pipe out and inspect the seal — if the O-ring is undamaged, push the pipe home fully until it reaches the pipe stop inside the fitting. Mark the insertion depth with a pencil before withdrawing if uncertain.
